using.explanation: Imports a namespace so you can use its types without writing the full name, or (as a statement) ensures a resource is properly disposed of.

namespace.explanation: Groups related classes together under a named container to organize code and avoid naming collisions.

class.explanation: Defines a blueprint for creating objects that share fields (data) and methods (behavior).

interface.explanation: Declares a set of members (methods, properties) that implementing classes are required to provide, without any implementation itself.

struct.explanation: Defines a lightweight value type (copied by value rather than by reference), typically used for small data groupings.

public.explanation: An access modifier that makes a class, method, or field accessible from anywhere.

private.explanation: An access modifier that makes a member accessible only from inside the same class.

protected.explanation: An access modifier that makes a member accessible within the same class and any subclasses.

internal.explanation: An access modifier that makes a member accessible only from within the same assembly (project).

static.explanation: Marks a member as belonging to the class itself, rather than to individual instances of it.

void.explanation: Used as a method's return type to indicate it doesn't return any value.

new.explanation: Creates a new instance (object) from a class or struct.

this.explanation: Refers to the current object instance a method is running on, often used to distinguish a field from a parameter of the same name.

base.explanation: Refers to the parent class, used to call its constructor or an overridden member from a subclass.

virtual.explanation: Marks a method as able to be overridden by a subclass.

override.explanation: Provides a new implementation for a virtual (or abstract) member inherited from a base class.

sealed.explanation: Marks a class so it can't be inherited from, or a method so it can't be overridden any further.

abstract.explanation: Marks a class that can't be instantiated directly, or a method with no body that subclasses must implement.

if.explanation: Runs a block of code only when a condition is true.

else.explanation: Runs a block of code when the preceding if (or else if) condition was false.

for.explanation: A loop that repeats a block of code a set number of times, using a counter variable (init; condition; step).

foreach.explanation: Loops directly over the elements of a collection or array, one at a time.

while.explanation: Repeats a block of code as long as a condition stays true, checked before each iteration.

do-while.explanation: Like a while loop, but the code runs once before the condition is checked, so it always executes at least once.

switch.explanation: Compares a value against several possible cases and runs the matching block.

case.explanation: Defines one branch to match against inside a switch statement.

break.explanation: Immediately exits the nearest loop or switch statement.

continue.explanation: Skips the rest of the current loop iteration and moves on to the next one.

return.explanation: Exits a method and optionally sends a value back to whoever called it.

try.explanation: Marks a block of code to attempt running, so that if it throws an exception, you can catch and handle it instead of crashing.

catch.explanation: Runs when the matching try block throws a matching exception type, giving you access to the exception object.

finally.explanation: Runs after the try/catch, whether or not an exception occurred — useful for cleanup like closing resources.

throw.explanation: Raises an exception on purpose, stopping normal execution unless it's caught by a matching catch block.

async.explanation: Marks a method as asynchronous, meaning it can use 'await' inside it to pause for other async operations without blocking the thread.

await.explanation: Pauses an async method until a Task completes, then gives you its result.

var.explanation: Lets the compiler infer a local variable's type from its initializer, instead of writing the type explicitly.

null.explanation: Represents the intentional absence of an object reference — a variable pointing to 'nothing'.

enum.explanation: Defines a fixed set of named constant values, useful when a variable should only ever be one of a few known options.
enum.example:
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
var c = Color.Green;

delegate.explanation: Defines a type that represents a reference to a method with a specific signature, allowing methods to be passed around like values.
delegate.example:
delegate int MathOp(int a, int b);
MathOp add = (a, b) => a + b;

record.explanation: A concise way to declare an immutable data-holding type with built-in value-based equality (C# 9+).
record.example:
record Point(int X, int Y);
var p = new Point(3, 4);

LINQ.explanation: Language-Integrated Query — a set of built-in methods/syntax for querying, filtering, and transforming collections directly in C#.
LINQ.example:
var xs = new[] { 1, 2, 3, 4 };
var evens = xs.Where(x => x % 2 == 0);
